text: The lateral dissection for transabdominal preperitoneal hernia repair can be tricky. Transitioning from an open approach to this operation does not provide any familiar landmarks either. I hope this video is helpful. This gentleman had a symptomatic left inguinal hernia and only mild discomfort on the right. The dissection begins below the arcuate line, so we should not encounter the posterior rectus fascia.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum sharply using electrocautery. Traction is applied in the posterior and medial direction, allowing insufflation to enter the dissection plane and facilitate its development. The dissection is maintained within the layers of the transverse allus fascia, the properitoneal layer. The surgeon dissects inferiorly for a short distance before extending the flap laterally to avoid operating in a tunnel.

The surgeon carefully dissects the flap, ensuring it is neither too shallow nor too deep to avoid creating a hole in the peritoneal flap or invading the retro-rectus plane. The dissection progresses posteriorly, avoiding lateral movement, to prevent exposure of the rectus abdominal muscle and associated vessels and nerves.

The surgeon uses scissors pointed downward to bluntly push laterally, preserving the dissection plane and the fat layer overlying the nerves. The video highlights the area above a small splotch of blood, indicating the dissection plane leading towards the nerves and the triangle of pain. The correct plane remains close to the flap, maintaining a layer of fat between the mesh and the nerves.

Following the completion of the lateral dissection, the surgeon proceeds to dissect medially towards the deep inguinal ring. The preserved layer overlying the abdominal wall muscle and the inferior epigastric vessels is visible, indicating the correct dissection plane.

The surgeon fast-forwards past the dissection of the hernia sac and medial dissection, focusing on the final portion of the lateral dissection. The gonadal vessels are freed from the lateral peritoneal flap to create space for the mesh, using blunt or sharp dissection techniques while avoiding electrocautery.
The surgeon deploys a 10 by 15 centimeter pro grip mesh into the surgical pocket, ensuring it fits adequately without any folds or kinks.
I hope this video was helpful. Thank you for watching.
